Qs.Headline & Outlet Metadata:
Populate this block only with externally verifiable facts, **not opinions**. Include:
• Headline text
• Publication/outlet name
• Publication date in ISO format (YYYY‑MM‑DD) – if missing, write "Unknown"
• Author(s) full names as credited
• Outlet section (e.g., Politics, Opinion)
• Outlet ownership/funding structure (parent company, nonprofit, etc.)
• Most widely‑cited media‑bias label (Left, Center‑left, Center, Center‑right, Right, Mixed) – cite the chart/source you used.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “The masthead shows POLITICO (generally rated center‑left by Ad‑Fontes). It’s dated 2025‑04‑21 and authored by Marianne Levine…”
Qs.Author/Outlet Background:
Summarize potential bias signals **not contained in the article text itself**:
• Author academic or professional background, previous beats, notable ideological statements, past fact‑check records.
• Any public controversies or conflicts of interest.
• Outlet editorial stance history, ownership influence, press‑freedom ranking, past retractions.
If reliable information cannot be located, explicitly note: "No additional background found."

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Lead author covered GOP primaries since 2018; no major fact‑check failures recorded. Outlet is owned by Axel Springer, outspokenly pro‑EU…”
Qs.Primary Claims Table:
Create a **markdown table** with columns:
| # | Claim (paraphrase) | Speaker (if any) | Claim Type (Fact / Statistic / Causal / Opinion) | Evidence Given in Article? (Y/N) | Initial Verifiability (High/Med/Low) |
Identify every statement that meaningfully shapes the reader’s perception of the political issue—even if presented as an aside.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Claim 1: ‘The bill will cut the deficit by $100 B’ (statement from Senator X)… likely statistical, article cites CBO report → Evidence Y → Verifiability High.”
Qs.Source Provenance Grid:
List **every** source that the article cites, quotes, or links. Use a five‑column markdown table:
| # | Citation Snippet / Link | Source Type (official doc, press conf, leaked memo, tweet, think‑tank report …) | First‑hand? (Original/Secondary) | Reliability (1‑5) |
Score reliability using mainstream fact‑checking standards (5 = peer‑reviewed or official gov record, 1 = anonymous forum post).

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Cites ‘Senate Appropriations Summary PDF’ → government doc → Original → 5.”
Qs.Bias Indicators:
Provide a **bullet list** of textual or structural cues that may signal bias. Do not judge validity—just describe what you see.
Possible angles to scan: emotive adjectives, imbalance of quoted voices, headline framing, image choice, omission of time‑line context, sensational leads, cherry‑picked statistics.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Uses ‘rammed through’ instead of ‘passed’, quotes 3 supporters vs 0 detractors, places dissenting data in final paragraph…”
Qs.Cross‑Checks:
Using your IR snippets, supply **at least three** external sources that either **corroborate** or **contradict** key claims. Present in a markdown table:
| # | External Source (outlet/title) | Relationship (Supports / Contradicts) | Brief Notes |
If no relevant external confirmation is found, state that explicitly.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Reuters piece published same day cites identical deficit figure (supports). Brookings analysis questions methodology (contradicts)…”
